coredata.dc:title: A novel Doyle-Fuller-Newman battery model formulation for online parameter estimation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gotti, Davide
Prodanovic, Milan
Pinilla, Sergio
Muñoz-Torrero, David
García-Quismondo, Enrique
Palma, Jesús
coredata.dc:description:
This work proposes a new formulation of the Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model of an electrochemical cell by solving in an alternative manner the partial differential equations of the electrode potentials. This allows the user to run the model with shorter computational times and to capture the capacitive behavior of electrochemical cells by using a single simulation framework. Moreover, a novel parameter estimation method is proposed to enhance the accuracy of the model. Furthermore, the method allows extracting the meaningful information regarding the relationship between the relevant parameters of the model and the operating conditions of the cell. This work considers a Lithium-ion cell NMC111/Graphite pouch cell, and the accuracy of the approach is validated using laboratory galvanostatic measurement tests at different C-rate values and with a realistic Federal Urban Driving Schedule test (FUDS). The proposed method not only shows minimal computational times, but also displays high estimation accuracy. In fact, with the proposed online parameter estimator, the mean relative and mean absolute errors are around 0.1% and
6 – 12
mV
, respectively, highlighting the robustness and high accuracy of the proposed model under a broad range of experimental conditions.

head.ce:title: A novel Doyle-Fuller-Newman battery model formulation for online parameter estimation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work proposes a new formulation of the Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model of an electrochemical cell by solving in an alternative manner the partial differential equations of the electrode potentials. This allows the user to run the model with shorter computational times and to capture the capacitive behavior of electrochemical cells by using a single simulation framework.

Moreover, a novel parameter estimation method is proposed to enhance the accuracy of the model. Furthermore, the method allows extracting the meaningful information regarding the relationship between the relevant parameters of the model and the operating conditions of the cell. This work considers a Lithium-ion cell NMC111/Graphite pouch cell, and the accuracy of the approach is validated using laboratory galvanostatic measurement tests at different C-rate values and with a realistic Federal Urban Driving Schedule test (FUDS). The proposed method not only shows minimal computational times, but also displays high estimation accuracy. In fact, with the proposed online parameter estimator, the mean relative and mean absolute errors are around 0.1% and
6 – 12
mV
, respectively, highlighting the robustness and high accuracy of the proposed model under a broad range of experimental conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model, also known as the pseudo two-dimensional model, has been firstly proposed in [1]. It has gained popularity in recent years due to its high physical fidelity and the ability to provide the user with a good resolution of several internal processes of the battery, such as diffusion, charge conservation, reaction kinetics, among others. Furthermore, it provides accurate performance predictions for several principal battery variables such as the cell voltage, the state of charge (SoC), and internal concentration gradients. The DFN is a complex model, composed of several non-linear partial differential equations (PDEs) that are tightly coupled with each other. It requires a significant computational burden, and this has prevented it from being used in many real-time applications. However, with the increased computational power of modern PCs and solvers, its usefulness for battery management system (BMS) applications has been recently unfolded [2,3]. In light of these considerations, this work proposes a new DFN model formulation that is computationally suitable for BMS applications. Furthermore, this work presents an online parameter estimator to further increase the accuracy and reliability of the DFN model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Literature review of parameter identification method for battery models
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, significant efforts have been made to enhance the performance of the DFN model by estimating its parameters in a more accurate and generalized manner.

For instance, [4] proposed the usage of an extended Kalman filter for estimating the SoC of a battery and the electrode-average solid concentration at the solid-particle surface. This was a seminal attempt to employ Kalman filter variants for online parameter estimation of the DFN model. The estimation of the SoC was increasingly accurate, with reported percentile errors around 3%. However, only a couple of internal variables could be estimated, as this approach was applicable only to the PDE of the solid-particle concentration of the DFN model. Moreover, the only test that was considered is the so-called FreedomCar test, which only lasts 60 s and does not allow ample variations of the voltage cell. Thus, the method was assessed only under a relatively limited operation range.

Ref. [5] proposed an
H
∞
filter to estimate the open circuit voltage and the SoC of a Lithium-ion battery. Reported errors on the SoC estimation were below 2%, and the approach was robust to high measurement noise levels and had small computational burdens. However, as the method was applied to a first-order RC equivalent circuit, it could not capture the fast dynamics of the battery or internal electrochemical phenomenon. Its applicability to a physics-based model was not explored. Furthermore, the method has only been demonstrated on a simple charge cycle; thus, its applicability to galvanostatic discharge cycles and more realistic operation conditions has not yet been evaluated.

In [6], different techniques were used to carry out the parameter identification of the DFN model. Firstly, a sensitivity analysis based on the Latin-Hypercube sampling method was used under different C-rate conditions. Once the most relevant parameters were identified, an initial guess of their values was provided using a long short-term memory neural network. This initial guess was used by a genetic algorithm-based optimizer to identify parameters under low and moderate C-rate tests. Finally, the remaining parameters were estimated using an ensemble Kalman filter. This work presented a novel framework for DFN parametrization and paved the way for further research efforts in this regard. Nevertheless, according to reported results, the estimation accuracy for some parameters was not sufficiently high. For instance, the estimation of the relative error of the cathode and anode reaction rate constant was about 14%, and the cathode solid-particle diffusion coefficient estimation was around 24%.

The method proposed in [7] allows taking into consideration the hysteresis effect, which is particularly relevant for
LiFePO
4
batteries, improving the accuracy of the cell voltage provided by the model. The hysteresis effect was modeled as a constant offset value over the open-circuit potential of the cell. This constant value was obtained by taking the average between the measured charge and the discharge cell voltage. The target thermodynamic parameter calculation was used to fit the simulated cell voltage with the aforementioned average cell voltage value, thus discounting the negative effect of the hysteresis. Afterwards, highly sensitive parameters of the DFN model were identified by the sensitivity analysis, and they were estimated using the particle Swarm optimization method. For the initial SoC estimation, a Bayesian optimization process was implemented. While the approach provided accurate results with reported average error metrics between 6–20 mV, the reliability of this method was not completely assessed as it was implemented only under galvanostatic conditions.

A fast parameter identification employing a model-assisted Bayesian optimization was proposed in [8]. To avoid numerical stability issues when tuning the DFN model with unfeasible parameter values, the authors proposed using a support vector machine to categorize which parameters could lead to stability problems during the simulation, thus avoiding their use. The accuracy provided by this method was higher than that of other estimation methods, such as the genetic algorithm, particle swarm optimization, and teaching-learning-based optimization algorithms. Furthermore, it was tested on both galvanostatic and urban dynamometer driving schedule tests, thus covering a wide range of battery working conditions. The average error was within an acceptable range of 5–20 mV. The main drawback of this approach was that, like most other data-driven approaches, it required high-quality measurements across a wide range of working conditions to properly extract all the parameters. Furthermore, if the support vector machine fails to categorize some faulty parameters, the accuracy of the overall estimation framework might decrease significantly.

The method proposed in [9] carried out a sensitivity analysis of the DFN model under different SoC and temperature conditions by using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test in a range of [
1
mHz – 1
kHz
]. Once the relevant parameters were selected, a “Grey Wolf” optimization method was used to determine their values. This work conducted an EIS test on the DFN model, which was not addressed in other works, and it reported highly accurate impedance estimations, compared to experimental values. However, the estimated DFN parameters were identified solely for EIS test conditions, and the parametrized DFN model was not tested under other conditions, such as galvanostatic or FUDS tests, which are more representative of real-life operating conditions of the battery.

The Ref. [10] proposed a multi-category parameter sensitivity analysis under several working conditions of a Li
+
battery. After the sensitivity analysis, a two-population multiple objective ”Grey Wolf” optimization algorithm was used, which increased the accuracy of the parameter estimation compared to the standard GWO by using non-converging parameters. One of the main advantages of this method was that it identified which tests need to be done to identify the parameters of the DFN model that act on different dynamics. Furthermore, it proposed a method that allows the non-convergent parameters to be optimized over a wider optimization spectrum and, therefore, to improve the overall estimation accuracy compared to other methods.

This article proposes a novel DFN model formulation suitable for online parameter estimation. Unlike some of the aforementioned works, the method proposed in this article does not need to be trained with measurements taken under a wide range of working conditions, as it is not based on machine learning techniques. In fact, it exhibits robustness across comprehensive experimental campaigns, such as galvanostatic, FUDS, and EIS tests, using the same model parametrization. On the contrary, simulation results reported by several of the aforementioned proposals are derived under a limited range of working conditions. Thus, their generality is not always assessed. Furthermore, previous work does not deal with the SoC-dependent nature of some DFN parameters, such as the kinetic rate constants and the solid particle diffusion coefficient, treating them as constant parameters. This simplification prevents these methods from accurately capturing the cell voltage, especially in the final stage of the voltage drop.

This work proposes a new DFN model formulation by solving the boundary conditions of the electrode potential PDEs in a novel manner. With this adjustment, the model becomes suitable for its implementation in an online parameter estimator, which opens up a new set of possibilities. The main contributions of this work are summarized below.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
This work proposes an innovative approach to solve the boundary conditions of the PDEs of the potentials using the information from an equivalent second-order RC-circuit. The advantage of this formulation is twofold:
–
It captures the electrical capacitive behavior of the cell, thus carrying out Galvanostatic and Potentiostatic EIS (GEIS and PEIS) tests in a single simulation framework. In this regard, it is worth mentioning that several software packages currently available do not possess this feature [11–13];
–
The proposed formulation allows the model to be solved using reduced CPU time, a highly desirable characteristic for in situ applications such as monitoring and control within battery management systems (BMS).

Thanks to the aforementioned low computational burden of the proposed formulation, an online state estimator can be implemented. Specifically, this work proposes a square-root unscented Kalman filter (SR-UKF) to estimate sensitive DFN parameters during the operation of the cell. This proposal implies several advantages:
–
The accuracy of the DFN model is marginally increased, particularly during the last voltage decay;
–
It allows monitoring of how certain parameters vary with the operating conditions of the cell. For instance, this work demonstrates how to extract the dependence of the kinetic reaction rates of the model with respect to the SoC.
–
The application of the online parameter estimator allows the BMS to detect internal faults or abnormal degradation effects.

ce:section.ce:para: The rest of this document is organized as follows. Section 2 provides the reader with a brief description of the algorithms implemented in this work, namely, the DFN model formulation, the sensitivity analysis, and the SR-UKF employed for online parameter estimation. Section 3 delineates the overall methodology proposed in this article. Section 4 introduces the study case details, such as the characteristics of the electrochemical cell selected for this work, the discretization approach for the DFN model, the laboratory measurements, etc. In Section 5, the performance of the proposed method is tested using several case studies. Finally, the conclusion of this work is drawn in Section 6.
In this section, a brief mathematical description of the DFN model and the algorithms used to estimate its parameters is reported.
Since its first introduction, the DFN model has gained considerable popularity due to its high accuracy and the detailed resolution it provides to the user [1].

The list of the DFN model parameters, the state and algebraic variables, and the discretization variables is provided in Table 1 . For a complete overview of the model, the interested reader is redirected to [14].

It is well-known that the DFN model does not have an algebraic solution over time as the PDEs (2) and (5) are solely composed of Neumann BCs and do not present a time derivative. Most methods assume a potential at a certain spatial point as the reference potential of the battery (e.g., the potential at the anode current-collector might be conventionally assumed null
ϕ
s
n
( x = 0 , t )
= 0
) and solve the other potential PDEs by using an iterative method (typically Newton–Raphson) that infers the BC values based on the value of other state and algebraic variables. As iterative methods act as an external loop on the overall DFN framework, they make the DFN computationally expensive due to the computation of the Jacobian matrix and the need to solve the DFN model on potentially several iterations for each time step before reaching convergence.

In this work, we propose an alternative way of solving the DFN model. The value of the BCs of the electrode potentials is determined using a second-order equivalent RC circuit, which makes use of the open circuit potential (OPC) coming from the DFN model (determined knowing the concentration in the electrode solid particles
C
s
n , p
). Once the OCP of each electrode is provided by the DFN model, the cell OCP is determined as
O C
P
c e l l
= O C
P
p
− O C
P
n
. This information is then used by the 2nd-order RC circuit to provide the sought BC values
ϕ
s
n
( x = 0 , t )
and
ϕ
s
p
( x = L , t )
, thereby avoiding the need for iterative methods that would otherwise increase the computational burden of the model and slow down the calculations.

The parameters that compose the 2nd-order RC circuit are depicted in Fig. 1 .
R
s
is the battery series resistance, and it mostly accounts for the resistance of the electrolyte and the contacts.
R
d i f f
and
C
d i f f
take into consideration the diffusion effect that occurs inside solid particles by including the slow transient related to the diffusion phenomenon. Finally, the branch composed of
R
d l
and
C
d l
is used to include faster transients associated with the double-layer capacity at the electrode-electrolyte interface and charge transfer resistance [16]. This particular circuit is selected because it is one of the most widely used models for the electrical behavior representation of Lithium-ion batteries. It effectively captures both the dynamic and static response of the battery, requiring minimal computational effort. Furthermore, it offers a straightforward interpretation of the results, making it user-friendly and practical for a range of applications. It is worth mentioning that higher-order RC equivalent circuits, composed of a higher number of parameters, would increase the complexity of parameter identification and would imply higher computational times, without a significant increase in accuracy for short- and mid-term studies compared to the aforementioned 2nd-order RC circuit [16]. However, for long-term studies, which are out of the scope of this work, this circuit presents some limitations since its time-constant parameters are not able to capture certain phenomena, such as the time- and cycle-dependent nature of SEI growth. For such applications, a more detailed equivalent circuit is likely needed.

One interesting by-product of this approach is the capability of the proposed DFN model to capture electrical capacitive behavior. In this regard, it is worth mentioning that most of the commercially available software cannot carry out an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test on the DNF model, as this test is typically carried out on an equivalent RC-circuit model. By doing so, the model does not provide the user with the capability to observe how the cell states, such as the concentrations and the potentials of the electrode and the electrolyte, evolve during this test. However, with the proposed model framework, this disadvantage is circumvented. This can be explained considering that the iterative process used to solve the aforementioned BCs does not consider the electrical link between electrode potentials and the applied current; for this reason, the traditional way of solving the DFN model is unsuitable for EIS analysis. A numerical example reported in Section 5.3 shows the capability of the proposed DFN model to carry out this type of test.

It is worth mentioning that the scope of the proposed model is not limited to the determination of the electrode potentials. In fact, the approach described in this subsection is used to extract their boundary condition value, avoiding computationally expensive operations. However, this process does not limit the use of the other PDEs. Thus, the proposed model provides the user with the same resolution regarding the battery states (
C s
,
ϕ s
,
C e
,
ϕ e
, and
i e
) as the standard DFN model.
The sensitivity analysis is used to evaluate how the output of a model is influenced by its parameters and states.

Firstly, each parameter and state that needs to be analyzed is sampled. Among the most common sampling techniques, one can find the Monte Carlo approach (each input is sampled independently from its distribution), Latin Hypercube (it makes use of the so-called stratified sampling as it divides the input range into intervals), Sobol sequence (it uniformly fills the input range using low-discrepance sequences). For further details, the interested reader can refer to [17].

Once this process is carried out, the sampling vectors serve as input for the sensitivity analysis, and the model is run. At this stage, there are several sensitivity methods that can be chosen [17]. For instance, there are local sensitivity approaches that consider the model output changes near a nominal set of input values. They are simple and do not require high computational burdens. However, they are not accurate under non-linear conditions of the model. Global methods perform the analysis over the entire sample range and make use of statistical or variance-based methods to evaluate the parameter influence. These methods are robust, accurate, and suitable for non-linear models, at the cost of high computational costs [18].

Once the analysis is completed, several metrics can be used to assess the influence magnitude of the parameters and states. Some of them are the correlation coefficient, the standardized regression, the partial correlation, and the Sobol’s index, among others [18]. In this work, only the standardized regression and the partial correlation are used to analyze the results of the sensitivity process. The correlation coefficient, which is representative of the linear relationship between a parameter and the output, frequently provides misleading results as it does not account for interactions between parameters, which is a limiting factor in a high-dimensional model such as the DFN. For these reasons, it is not reported in this work. On the other hand, the standardized regression provides the relative impact of each parameter on the output, controlling for other parameters. However, this metric needs to be coupled with others since it assumes a linear relationship between parameters and the output, which is not always the case. For this reason, the partial correlation metric is also used, as it provides the relationship between a parameter and another after removing the effect of other inputs. This metric is often used for detecting pure association, and it is less affected by collinearity than the standardized regression. Therefore, considering these two metrics together provides a quite complete overview of the sensitivity hyperspace.

Recently, a class of Kalman filters based on sigma-point sampling methods has attracted much attention in the field of state and parameter estimation. They make use of the unscented transformation [19], which employs a set of deterministically chosen points to approximate a probability distribution. Among this Kalman filter class, one can find the square root unscented Kalman filter (SR-UKF), which is a variant of the well-known UKF that guarantees positive semi-definiteness of the state covariance, thus providing better numerical stability properties and robustness [20].

It is worth noting that the standard Kalman filter cannot be applied to parameter estimation in nonlinear models such as the DFN. Instead, a nonlinear state and parameter estimator must be employed. In particular, this variant of the UKF is chosen over other state and parameter estimation algorithms, such as the extended Kalman filter or the nonlinear weighted least squares, due to its high accuracy, robustness to measurement noise, remarkable numerical stability properties, and its capability of preserving the first two statistical moments up to at least the second order of accuracy for any nonlinear system - a desirable feature when dealing with a highly nonlinear model such as the DFN. Moreover, it requires a low computational cost as it does not require the computation of the Jacobian of the observation function for the linearization process. For a quantitative comparison between the SR-UKF and other state-of-the-art parameter estimation methods, the interested reader is redirected to [21].

This section provides a brief resume of the methodology used in this work to parametrize the DFN model and to carry out the proposed online parameter estimation. Specifically, it shows the interaction between the DFN model formulation, the sensitivity analysis, and the SR-UKF online parameter estimator, whose mathematical background is described in Section 2.

At the initial stage of the proposed approach, once the battery to be studied is selected, the data provided by the manufacturer is consulted and used; they typically include geometric parameters such as the thickness of the cathode, anode, and separator, and the cell area. Then, other parameters are obtained from references in the literature that employ the same chemistries. These include the electrode, electrolyte, and miscellaneous parameters reported in Table 1. Furthermore, it is necessary to select the discretization methods to solve the PDEs of the DFN model, along with the spatial discretization grid and the time integration step. This process is detailed in Section 4. Afterwards, the model is subject to preliminary testing to verify the absence of numerical stability problems and to ensure it provides coherent, even if not fully accurate, results. If this is achieved, this parametrization is adopted as a starting point.

Subsequently, laboratory experiments such as galvanostatic steps, variable charge–discharge power pulses (the automotive industry standard Federal Urban Driving Schedule, FUDS), and EIS tests are conducted on the battery cells selected for this work. These tests are then used to check if the behavior of the parametrized model indeed adheres to the real battery.

At this point, a sensitivity analysis test, as carried out in Section 5.1.1, is conducted to understand which parameters are more sensitive under certain working conditions. The key idea is to estimate the parameters that have a higher impact on the model output, and to preserve the value from the literature for the others. In fact, irrelevant or negligible parameters will retain their pre-assigned values (typically provided from the manufacturer or from literature references), while the most relevant parameters will undergo an optimization process to further improve the model accuracy. This approach is adopted because the DFN model comprises a considerable number of parameters, making the simultaneous optimization of all of them impractical and time-consuming. Furthermore, model accuracy is generally sensitive to only a small subset of these parameters.

At this stage, an optimization process is applied to estimate the most relevant parameters highlighted by the sensitivity analysis. This procedure is performed under certain operating conditions (e.g., a galvanostatic test with a certain C-rate), and the model is then assessed under other operating conditions to verify the generality of the parametrization. If this is achieved, the offline parameter estimation is concluded. The duration of the optimization process might extend to several hours. The implementation details of this process are reported in Section 5.1.2.

Finally, to further improve the accuracy of the model, some parameters are identified by using an online estimator, i.e., they are not assumed to be constant during the simulation, but are left free to vary such that the
V
c e l l
of the DFN model adheres, as much as possible, with the measured
V
c e l l
ˆ
. In this work, this is achieved by employing an SR-UKF, which requires the applied current
I
a p p
as input signal, and the cell voltage
V
c e l l
as observation. Details of calibration and implementation of the SR-UKF, along with considerations regarding observability issues, are provided in Section 5.1.3.
The flowchart shown in Fig. 2 graphically resumes the proposed approach.

The modified version of the DFN model described in Section 2 is implemented considering a Lithium-ion cell. Particularly, all the study cases reported in this article are conducted on a Lithium-ion pouch cell 402035-size manufactured by Li-Fun Technology (Xinma Industry Zone, Golden Dragon Road, Tianyuan District, Zhuzhou City, Hunan Province, PRC, 412000), i.e., a Li[

Most of the DFN parameters reported in Table 1 are taken from [22], as their values provide the model with a coherent and stable behavior. However, using these parameters, the model is not accurate. Thus, to achieve a good coherence of the model with laboratory measurements, some parameters are estimated through an optimization process. The selection of the parameters worth optimizing is motivated by means of a sensitivity analysis. Both the optimization and the sensitivity analysis applied to the Lithium-ion NMC111/graphite cell are duly discussed in subsequent sections.

Regarding the discretization method used to solve the DFN model, the Crank–Nicolson method is applied to the concentration PDEs of the electrode and electrolyte (Eqs. (1) and (3), respectively). The PDEs of the electrode and electrolyte potentials (Eqs. (2) and (5), respectively) and of the electrolyte current (Eq. (4) are discretized using the central difference approximation method. These finite-element methods are chosen over other discretization approaches as they allow the DFN to be formulated with a state-space representation and with smaller CPU times. It is probably worth remarking that these two characteristics are necessary to implement an unscented Kalman filter to carry out an online parameter estimation of the model. For further details on the accuracy, numerical stability properties, and computational burden of this discretization approach applied to the DFN, the interested reader can refer to the following comparison study between different discretization approaches of the DFN model [23].

100

, which corresponds to the sampling time of the respective measurement time series. In this work, the cell temperature is supposed to have a constant value of 25 °C. This is a reasonable assumption as the cell is tested in a climatic chamber that sets the temperature at 25 °C, and all the tests are carried out with a relatively small C-rate current [24].

In this work, galvanostatic and FUDS tests are conducted. This implies that the applied current

is imposed on the cell. Therefore, from the point of view of the model, it acts as an input, whereas the cell voltage varies accordingly to the internal cell dynamics and is therefore treated as the model output. For this reason, the voltage cell provided by the DFN model is compared to the one measured in the laboratory to assess the accuracy of the proposed approach. More specifically, to provide the user with a quantitative evaluation of the model accuracy, the following error metrics are used in this work: the root mean square error (RMSE), mean of the relative errors (MRE), and the mean absolute errors (MAE) are used as comparison metrics. These metrics are used to detect different type of errors, each offering distinct advantages. The RMSE emphasizes larger errors due to the square operation, which is desirable when evaluating a model’s worst-case performance. The MRE metric provides a percentage-base indication of the error, offering insight into its relative magnitude and direction. Finally, the MAE provides a more balanced view of the overall error compared to the RMSE as it does not disproportionately weight outliers.
The information regarding the calibration of the SR-UKF is provided in the related study case sections, as it varies from case to case.

The DFN model and the associated SR-UKF parameter estimation algorithm are manually coded in MATLAB-Simulink environment. The sensitivity study and the parameter optimization process are carried out using the “Sensitivity Analysis” and “Parameter Estimation” toolboxes available in MATLAB. The PC used in this work is an 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1135G7 @ 2.40 GHz 1.38 GHz processor, with 16 GB RAM memory and a 64-bit operating system.

In this section, numerical results are provided. Firstly, galvanostatic tests with 0.5 and 1 C-rate are reported. Subsequently, to validate the procedure under a more realistic operating scenario, the proposed procedure is tested using the Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS) test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Galvanostatic tests at 0.5 C-rate and 1 C-rate

ce:section.ce:para:
As previously mentioned, most of the parameters of the DFN model are taken from [22] as this reference refers to a cell with the same electrochemistry (NMC111/Graphite) used in this work. However, with these parameters, the model does not provide sufficiently accurate results. Therefore, to further increase the accuracy of the DFN model, some of its parameters are optimized under the specific test conditions of this work. To detect which parameters have a greater effect on
V
c e l l
, and are therefore worth estimating, a sensitivity analysis is executed. For this purpose, the Sensitivity Analysis toolbox available in MATLAB has been used.

This test is carried out considering two consecutive discharge and charge cycles at 1 C-rate and 0.5 C-rate. All the electrochemical parameters of the DFN model, such as the reaction rate constants
k
n , p
and the electronic conductivity
σ
n , p
, are considered for the sensitivity analysis. Geometrical parameters, such as the thickness of the electrodes
L
n , p
and the cross-section area
A
c e l l
are not included as they are known. For each of these parameters, 100 samples are generated using the Latin Hypercube sampling method. This method is selected as it guarantees a better coverage of the input space, as each parameter sample is uniformly sampled, thus achieving an equal or greater statistical coverage with fewer samples than random sampling or other methods.

The results of the sensitivity analysis reported in Fig. 4 show the influence of the 20 most influential parameters on
V
c e l l
. As discussed in Section 2.2, only the standardized regression and the partial correlation are used in this work, as the correlation coefficient might provide misleading results in a highly non-linear model such as the DFN. It can be easily inferred that the anode and cathode kinetic reaction rate constants,
k
n
and
k
p
, respectively, are the most sensitive parameters under these operating conditions. Also, the anode and cathode diffusion constants,
D
s
n
and
D
s
p
, respectively, and the transference number
t
0
+
are identified as sensitive parameters.
Thus, to considerably improve the performance of the DFN model, these are the parameters that need to be estimated. This procedure is dealt with in the next subsection.

Apart from the five aforementioned parameters, due to the introduction of the second-order RC circuit proposed in this work, also the parameters
R
s
,
R
d i f f
,
C
d i f f
,
R
d l
,
C
d l
need to be identified. For this reason, the total number of parameters that need to be estimated is ten.

As mentioned in Section 3, the estimation is carried out by means of an optimization process. For this task, the Parameter Estimator toolbox available in the MATLAB environment is used. The cost function selected for this process is the Sum Squared Error, which minimizes the squared error of estimated
V
c e l l
with respect to measured
V
c e l l
ˆ
.

The model is initialized using the parameter values reported in [22], as they provide the model with a coherent behavior and seem a reasonable starting point. To expedite the optimization process and to avoid reaching physically implausible solutions, the parameters are bounded to certain reasonable limits, coherent with the electrochemical properties of the cell under study. Thus, the optimization problem to be formulated is a constrained one.

The chosen optimization method is the non-linear least squares, which showed better stability properties during the process compared to other methods, such as Gradient Descent and Pattern Search, which frequently remained stuck in a local minimum, providing inaccurate results.

The algorithm used is the Trust-Region-Reflective, which is selected for its outstanding stability properties in case of ill-conditioned problems. It is particularly robust for large and sparse problem (thus, it is effective for handling high-dimensional models such as the DFN) and, most importantly, it supports bound constraints [25]. On the other hand, the other algorithm available in the toolbox is Levenberg–Marquardt, which is applicable only to unconstrained problems and, therefore, is not suitable for this task [25].

The optimization process leads to the values reported in the last column of Table 2 . To guarantee repetitiveness, the initial guess and the boundaries used during the optimization process are also reported. To provide a full account, the parameters of the DFN model used in this work are reported in Table A.1.

After the optimization process has converged to a stable and feasible solution, the model provides coherent results for all the discharge and charge cycles at 1 and 0.5 C-rate. In fact, observing Fig. 5 , the model shows good accuracy throughout the whole simulation. However, it cannot properly detect the final voltage dip of the discharge cycles and the initial voltage rise of the charge phase. This is a common issue with the DFN, as it is quite challenging to tune it in a generalized manner. As a matter of fact, if tuned to match very precisely a certain operating condition (e.g., a charge cycle with a certain C-rate value), it provides incoherent results for other cases.

Table 3 provides a more quantitative view of the accuracy achieved by the model after this tuning process. All the errors are non-negligible, in particular the RMSE, whose relatively high value depends on the fact that, on several occasions, the model provides relatively high deviations from the actual voltage value.

To improve the performance of the model, the next subsection makes use of an online parameter estimator that is able to capture the varying nature of the kinetic reaction rates
k
p
and
k
n
with respect to the SoC of the cell.

As mentioned in Section 3, the SR-UKF is used to extract useful information regarding how reaction rate constants
k
n , p
need to vary to match
V
c e l l
. The rationale behind the selection of these two parameters is the following. It is well-known from the literature that both the reaction rate constants [26,27] and the diffusion coefficients [28,29] are indeed dependent on the SoC. However, the sensitivity analysis presented previously indicated the two reaction rate constants as the most relevant ones under galvanostatic conditions. This result seems coherent as the galvanostatic tests are conducted using moderate currents of 0.5 and 1 C-rate. Under these conditions, the time constant of the diffusion effect (the other strongly SoC-dependent phenomenon) of the anode is
τ

= 13 . 29
s
. They are both sensibly smaller than the discharge/charge cycles (
7392
s
and
3382
s
for 0.5 C-rate and 1 C-rate, respectively). Thus, it can be assumed that the variation of the concentration in the solid particles is considerably faster than the reaction current j driven by the reaction rate constants
k
n , p
. As the diffusion coefficients and the reaction rate constant act on different time scales, it is possible to discern them. Although the SR-UKF uses only one measurement, namely the
V
c e l l
, the system remains observable as the number of parameters to be estimated is limited. Thus, no observability issue arises.

With these considerations being drawn, this work makes use of an SR-UKF parameter estimator specifically targeting the reaction rate constants of the cathode and anode.
V

is the output of the parameter estimator, and

. This setting proved to be reliable and robust to several test conditions and was determined after a trial-and-error procedure.

Fig. 6 depicts the cell voltage during a discharge 0.5 C-rate cycle using fixed reaction rate values (i.e.,
k
n
= 1 . 09 ⋅ 1
0
− 5
and

obtained from the aforementioned optimization process) and variable parameters. To further illustrate the accuracy of the proposed method, the estimation error defined as
E r r o r =

is presented in the lower part of the figure. It can be observed how the parameter estimator enhances the accuracy of the model, allowing one to achieve highly accurate cell voltage estimations. In particular, the online parameter estimation allows to approximate the last voltage dip of the discharge cycle, which is of pivotal importance for preventing over-discharging and improving the lifespan of the battery. As shown in Fig. 7 , a similar result is obtained using a 1 C-rate cycle, showing the robustness of the approach.

In Table 4 , the error metrics are reported. It is straightforward to see that the proposed online parameter estimation approach has a significant impact on all the metrics. Noticeably, the RMSE is more than halved, showing that the error peaks are sensibly reduced, whereas the MRE and MAE are decreased by a factor of 3 or 4, indicating that the average error is also sensibly reduced.

The CPU times of the model with fixed parameters are practically negligible, smaller than

for both tests). This result is significant as the proposed DFN model variant that makes use of a 2nd-order equivalent RC circuit allows one to achieve significantly smaller CPU times with respect to other renowned softwares that employ the DFN model such as PyBaMM [12] (

As expected, the presence of the online parameter estimator increases the computational times of the model, from around

. However, the time required to carry out one time iteration is several orders of magnitude smaller than the typical time resolutions for BMS applications, which typically range from a few tens of ms up to some seconds [16]. This is a significant fact, as the integration of the parameter estimator does not jeopardize the usage of the model for real-time monitoring and control applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Galvanostatic cycles at 2 C-rate and 3 C-rate
ce:section.ce:para: In this subsection, the DFN model is tested under constant current cycles at 2 C-rate and 3 C-rate. The parameters of the DFN model are the same as before, which were determined through an optimization process over charge and discharge cycles at 0.5 and 1 C-rate, as described in Section 5.1.2. In this manner, the generality of the proposed approach is tested under higher current values.

Simulation results considering both fixed
k
p
and
k
n
and the presence of the SR-UKF online parameter estimator are reported in Fig. 8 and in Fig. 9 , for 2 C-rate and 3 C-rate discharge cycles, respectively. The accuracy achieved is comparable with that obtained under 0.5 C-rate and 1 C-rate, indicating that, as long as the discharge current is comparable with that used during the optimization process, the model provides coherent results.

The RMSE, MRE, and MAE metrics for these tests are reported in Table 5 . In case the online parameter estimator is not employed, coherent results are achieved. Nevertheless, as in the previous case study, the model is not very precise in detecting the last voltage decay. In case the proposed SR-UKF parameter estimator is used, the model accuracy is remarkably increased, reducing all the error metrics. The precision achieved using the SR-UKF is comparable with that of Section 5.1.3, demonstrating the versatility of the proposed online parameter estimator across different working conditions of the cell.

This subsection shows the capability of the proposed DFN model variant to capture the electrical capacitive behavior of the NMC111/ graphite cell used in this work. For this purpose, a GEIS test is simulated, considering an amplitude of 0.25 C-rate current and a frequency range of
[ 0 . 1
mHz – 1
kHz ]
, on the proposed DFN model parametrized as discussed in Section 5.1. Fig. 10 reports the related Nyquist plot.

It can be observed that, with the proposed approach, the DFN model has the capability to capture the electrical capacitive behavior of the cell. In the magnified portion of the figure, which considers results for relatively high frequency values of
[ 0 . 1
Hz – 1
kHz ]
, it can be observed that the cell shows mostly resistive behavior. Afterwards, as the frequency decreases, the cell impedance enters the so-called Warburg region (frequency smaller than
0 . 1
Hz
), with a typical strongly capacitive value that increases linearly. The capability to capture the electrical capacitive behavior of the cell seems to be an interesting feature of the proposed model, as it is able to link the internal electrochemical dynamics of the cell with its electrical dynamics observed from the current collectors. In fact, this feature allows the user to visualize the internal states of the cell (such as
C s
,
ϕ
s
,
C e
,
ϕ
e
, etc.) during EIS tests, which might be helpful for future studies or applications. The comparison of the simulation results with GEIS experiments lies beyond the scope of this article. However, owing to the promise this feature demonstrates, its suitability for these applications will be assessed in future work.

To assess the reliability of the proposed approach, the DFN model formulation and its parameter estimator are tested under the standard FUDS protocol. This test is commonly used to evaluate the battery performance in electric and hybrid vehicles under stop-and-go urban driving conditions. It includes a series of short-duration current pulses — potentially reaching up to 3C rates — that simulate rapid acceleration and regenerative braking events. These high-intensity pulses are interspersed with relaxation periods, representing the idle phases between acceleration and braking. For this case study, a 30-minute-long FUDS test is considered, ranging from an initial cell voltage of 3.61 V to 3.48 V.

As in the case of galvanostatic tests, the accuracy of the model using fixed parameters (coming from the aforementioned optimization problem) and the accuracy obtained with the proposed parameter estimator are compared.

V
c e l l
obtained from the DFN model that employs fixed parameters is depicted in red color in Fig. 11 . It can be observed that the estimations are relatively accurate and that not only is the proposed model valid under galvanostatic tests, but also under more realistic scenarios such as the FUDS test. However, the obtained results tend to be biased, particularly during the current pulses and the relaxation periods. This is not unexpected, as the cell undergoes rapid and substantial variations in state of charge (SoC) during these phases; thus, considering parameters such as
k
p
and
k
n
constant inevitably implies errors.

As before, we employ an SR-UKF that estimates the two reaction rate constants so that the voltage obtained from the model (
V
c e l l
) approximates the measured voltage (
V
c e l l
t
ˆ
) as best as possible. In this case study, the SR-UKF is set as follows. The process covariance matrix is
Q =
1
0
− 2
0
0
1
0
− 2
, the initial state covariance matrix is
P
00
=
1
0
− 4
0
0
1
0
− 4
, and the noise measurement covariance is
R = 1
0
− 5
. In this case, as the test requires higher reactivity from the model, the parameter estimator is set with smaller process covariance values (less confidence in static parameter values), and with smaller measurement covariance (higher confidence in the measurements, thus higher reactivity).

In Fig. 11, the estimations obtained employing the SR-UKF are reported. It is possible to observe that the performance of the model is sensibly enhanced. The model is able to capture the voltage pulses and relaxation periods.

This is reflected in the error metrics reported in Table 6 . The MRE is reduced by a factor of 4, indicating that the model that employs the online parameter estimation is not significantly biased in a certain direction. The RMSE and the MAE are also appreciably reduced, indicating that both the frequency and the magnitude of the peak of errors are reduced. Regarding the CPU times, similar considerations as in the previous study case can be drawn.

To further improve the accuracy of the DFN model, it is of importance to estimate, in an online manner, also cathode and anode diffusion coefficients
D
s
p
and
D
s
n
, respectively. This is of particular importance as these coefficients play an important role in the model’s behavior during relaxation periods (as the concentration of the solid particle varies). Being able to capture their SoC-varying dependency further improves the accuracy of the model. In this work, the inclusion of the diffusion coefficients into the parameter estimation vector was attempted. However, the results were not accurate. This fact can be explained by the fact that
V
c e l l
is the only available comparison metric for the SR-UKF. Therefore, if several parameters need to be estimated from a single observation, the system may become poorly observable or unobservable [30]. Thus, this remains an open question for future work, where a different parameter estimator may be used.
This subsection shows one possible application of the online parameter estimator proposed in this work. In this regard, results from Section 5.1 are used.

Fig. 12 reports the variation of reaction rate constants
k
p
and
k
n
with respect to the SoC value for the 1 C-rate and 0.5 C-rate galvanostatic cycles, computed by the proposed SR-UKF. It is possible to observe that under both cycle conditions, the curve is similar, in particular the one for
k
p
. Thus, one may consider to use this information to enhance the accuracy of the DFN model, without directly implementing the SR-UKF algorithm.

Specifically, the
k
p
and
k
n
vs SoC curves determined during a 0.5 C-rate cycle are incorporated into the DFN model in the shape of lookup tables during the 1 C-rate cycle. The results are reported in Fig. 13 . As expected, this approach is not as accurate as the one that uses the SR-UKF. However, it provides more accurate results than using the fixed reaction rate values. These considerations are reflected in Table 7 .

These results are relevant as they show that, when carrying out a galvanostatic test under moderate current conditions, the most relevant factor is the dependency of the reaction rate constants on the SoC. Otherwise, when used in other test conditions, the lookup table would have provided biased results as
k
p
and
k
n
determined by the SR-UKF would have been compensating for other internal dependencies (e.g.,
D
s
p
and
D
s
n
vs SoC, temperature dependencies, etc.). By reversing the approach, i.e., by using the reaction rate vs SoC information from the 1 C-rate cycle into the 0.5 C-rate cycle, similar results are obtained.
In future works, further tests will be conducted to validate these results under a wider range of working conditions of the cell.

To emphasize the contributions of the proposed work, a comparison with other pertinent parameter identification techniques is carried out in this subsection. In particular, Table 8 reports the average estimation error, the considered battery model, the parameter estimation approach, and the tests that have been used to validate the proposal.

It is probably worth reminding that, apart from providing high accuracy under a wide range of test conditions, the proposed approach can significantly reduce the computational times of the model and can allow the user to extract useful information regarding the parameters of the model, as shown in Section 5.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of spatial discretization grid on DFN accuracy
ce:section.ce:para:
This subsection analyzes the behavior of the proposed DFN model using different spatial discretization grids. It is worth remembering that all the aforementioned results are obtained using a number of discretization points
n
r
= 20
for the solid electrode particles, whereas
n
x
= 20
is used for the cell thickness.

In Fig. 14 , the results considering different
n
x
and
n
r
in the 1 C-rate galvanostatic test are reported. One may observe that increasing the number of discretization points for both the radial discretization direction of the solid particles and the thickness discretization direction to
n
r
= 30
and
n
x
= 30
, respectively, does not provide evident increments in the accuracy of the model. However, as reported in Table 9 , the CPU time suffers a sensible increase.

Reducing the number of discretization points may compromise the accuracy of the results. Specifically, since the configurations where
n
r
= 10
show the worst precisions, it has been detected that the most sensitive discretization direction is the radial one. The rationale behind this result can be explained by considering that the concentration gradient in the solid particles might be quite steep, particularly during fast charge and discharge or for low concentration values [14]. Whereas the potential of the electrodes and of the electrolyte presents a slower variation in space.

As
n
r
= 20
and
n
x
= 20
achieve an appropriate trade-off between accuracy and computational expense, this configuration is employed in the spatial discretization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of time integration step on DFN accuracy
ce:section.ce:para: The susceptibility of the proposed DFN model to the time integration step is assessed in this subsection.

As mentioned before, the integration interval
d t
used in this work is 400 ms for the galvanostatic tests and 100 ms for the FUDS test. These values are chosen to match the measurement time samples, which is a common choice when validating a model. However, due to some limitations of the measurement system or the signal channels, a higher
d t
might be implemented in real-world applications. For this reason, the accuracy of the model is assessed considering doubled and quadrupled time steps of 800 ms and 1600 ms.

Results on a galvanostatic discharge cycle at 0.5 C-rate, considering fixed
k
n
and
k
p
and the presence of the SR-UKF are reported in Fig. 15 and Fig. 16 , respectively. The related RMSE, MRE, and MAE errors are detailed in Table 10 .

It is possible to observe that the model remains quite accurate, particularly when the online parameter estimator is implemented. As expected, the higher the time integration step, the smaller the accuracy. This does not come as a surprise, as a larger
d t
introduces bigger errors when approximating with discrete steps the continuous process represented by the partial differential equation.

A smaller
d t
would help the model achieve even better accuracy. In this regard, it is worth reminding that the CPU times of the model are around a few ms. Thus, the proposed DFN framework is suitable to be run with sensibly smaller time integration steps, as long as the measurement sampling of the BMS allows it.

This paper proposes a novel way of solving the BCs of the DFN model. Specifically, the BCs of the electrode potential PDE are solved through a second-order RC equivalent circuit. In this manner, not only does the proposed model exhibit a lower computational burden, but it can also capture the capacitive behavior of the cell, which is not detectable by the standard DFN formulation. Additionally, thanks to the significant reduction in the computational burden of the model, an online parameter estimator can be implemented.

The results obtained on both galvanostatic and FUDS protocols show the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed modeling approach. The proposed online parameter estimator based on the SR-UKF leads to a substantial reduction in errors, achieving remarkably small MRE around 0.1%, and MAE in the range of 6–12 mV. It is worth highlighting that these high accuracies are achieved across a wide range of experimental campaigns, thus demonstrating the robustness of the proposed approach.

Moreover, the proposed SR-UKF online parameter estimator can capture the SoC-dependency of several DFN parameters, allowing for further improvement in the accuracy of the model. Specifically, it has been shown that the proposed approach allows extracting information regarding the SoC-dependency nature of the reaction rate constants, under test conditions where they are the most relevant parameters. In principle, this application may also be extended to other SoC-dependent parameters such as the diffusion coefficients. This feature seems of particular interest as it may open up a new range of research opportunities, such as the use of SoC-dependent parameter curves to enhance the standard DNF model, or the prediction of accelerated aging effects or internal faults.

Further work will be dedicated to the inclusion of the thermal differential equation into the proposed model framework, as the cell’s thermal dynamics may become significant in the case of higher C-rate tests or environments without controlled thermal conditions. Future work will address the estimation of other SoC-dependent parameters, such as the diffusion coefficients, to further increase the accuracy of the model under certain test types, to extract useful information regarding their SoC-dependency, and to validate results from laboratory tests (e.g., from the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique). To achieve this, a different parameter estimator capable of handling poorly observable systems, such as the DFN model, needs to be implemented.
